questions.question: Why is the `move` keyword needed when spawning a thread with a closure that uses external variables?
options: It transfers ownership of captured variables to the thread
questions.explanation: The spawned thread might outlive the scope where variables were created. `move` transfers ownership to the closure so references remain valid.

questions.question: Rust prevents data races at compile time through its ownership and type system.
questions.explanation: The Send and Sync traits, combined with ownership rules, prevent data races. Types that aren't thread-safe won't compile in concurrent contexts.

questions.question: Create a channel and send a message between threads:
questions.explanation: `mpsc::channel()` creates a multiple-producer, single-consumer channel. `tx` sends values, `rx` receives them.

questions.question: What does `Mutex<T>` provide?
options: Mutual exclusion — only one thread can access the inner data at a time
questions.explanation: Mutex ensures only one thread holds the lock at a time. `.lock()` returns a MutexGuard that auto-releases when dropped.

questions.question: Share a Mutex across threads using Arc:
questions.explanation: Arc::clone increments the atomic reference count. Each thread gets its own Arc handle to the shared Mutex.

questions.question: The Send trait marks types whose ownership can be transferred between threads.
questions.explanation: Send means ownership can move across thread boundaries. Almost all Rust types are Send. Rc<T> is a notable exception (use Arc<T> instead).
